planting.note: Sow seeds on the surface of moist, humus-rich soil in fall to allow cold stratification over winter. Do not cover seeds, as they require light to germinate. Best established by transplanting small divisions from existing colonies rather than starting from seed.

directions: Virginia Pennywort thrives in the deep shade of moist deciduous forests and requires a rich, organic soil with consistent moisture year-round. Scatter seeds directly on the forest floor in fall, pressing lightly into the leaf litter without burying them, as they need cold winter temperatures to break dormancy. This partially parasitic plant depends on mycorrhizal fungi associated with tree roots, so siting it near established oaks, beeches, or maples significantly improves establishment success. Avoid disturbing the soil around established plants, as the shallow root network is easily damaged.

planting.note: Sow seeds directly in fall into well-drained, rocky or gravelly soil in a partially shaded woodland edge or open glade. Seeds are very fine; mix with sand for even distribution and press lightly onto the soil surface without covering.

directions: Purple Bluet is well suited to lean, rocky soils at woodland edges and cedar glades where competition from aggressive grasses is naturally limited. Direct sow seeds in fall by scattering them onto a prepared, weed-free surface and pressing them gently into contact with the soil without burying them. Plants develop slowly in the first year, establishing a root system before putting on significant top growth, so mark planting areas carefully to avoid accidental disturbance. Once established, Purple Bluet self-seeds reliably and gradually spreads to form attractive clumps that bloom from mid-spring into early summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall into dry, well-drained, low-fertility soil in a sunny to partially shaded location. Seeds are minute; surface-sow and press lightly to ensure soil contact without covering. Cold winter exposure promotes spring germination.

directions: Longleaf Bluet is an excellent choice for dry rock gardens, gravelly slopes, and naturalized prairie or meadow plantings where its tolerance for poor, well-drained soils gives it a competitive advantage. Sow seeds in fall directly onto a raked, weed-suppressed seedbed, pressing them into the surface without cover, as light aids germination after winter cold stratification. Avoid amending soil with compost or fertilizer, as rich conditions favor weedy competitors and cause Longleaf Bluet to decline. Plants form tight clumps that bloom profusely in late spring and are long-lived when sited in a dry, open exposure with minimal soil disturbance.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring after last frost into shaded, moist forest soil. Scarify seeds by lightly nicking the seed coat or soaking in warm water for 24 hours before sowing to improve germination rates. Sow at a depth of approximately 1/4 inch.

directions: Naked-flowered Tick Trefoil is a distinctive woodland native that grows in dry to moist deciduous forest shade, where its low-lying leaf stems form a handsome ground-level rosette separate from the tall, arching flowering stalk that rises directly from the root crown. Scarify seeds by rubbing lightly on sandpaper or soaking overnight in warm water, then sow directly in the garden in spring after soil temperatures reach 65 degrees Fahrenheit. Choose a site with dappled to full shade and well-drained, slightly acidic soil with a good leaf litter layer to mimic natural forest conditions. As a nitrogen-fixing legume, this plant benefits surrounding woodland vegetation and requires no supplemental fertilization once established.
sources: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox

common_name: Pointleaf Tick Trefoil / Large-flowered Tick Trefoil

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in spring into moist, shaded woodland soil. Scarification by nicking the seed coat or soaking in warm water for 24 hours before sowing is recommended to break seed dormancy. Sow seeds approximately 1/4 inch deep.

directions: Pointleaf Tick Trefoil is a striking native legume for shaded ravines, moist woodland gardens, and naturalized forest understories, where its bold, whorled leaflets and tall racemes of rose-pink flowers make a strong visual statement from midsummer through fall. Sow scarified seeds directly in spring once soil temperatures are consistently above 65 degrees Fahrenheit, pressing them about 1/4 inch deep into moist, humus-rich soil in a partially shaded location. Plants grow slowly in the first year while developing deep roots and will reach flowering size by the second or third season. The sticky seed pods that form after bloom are a valuable food source for wildlife, and the plant serves as a larval host for several skipper and butterfly species, making it an excellent candidate for wildlife-focused woodland plantings.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall into moist, well-drained soil in a partially sunny woodland glade or forest edge. Surface sow or barely cover seeds, as they need light and cold stratification over winter to germinate reliably in spring.

directions: Russell's Wild Bergamot is a rare and regionally distinctive bee balm native to Ozark glades, open woodlands, and rocky forest edges, where it thrives in well-drained soils with dappled to partial sun. Sow seeds in fall by pressing them lightly onto a prepared soil surface in the planting site, as they benefit from natural cold stratification over winter and will germinate the following spring. Space plants 18 to 24 inches apart to ensure good air circulation, which helps maintain the superior mildew resistance this species displays compared to other Monarda. Once established, plants spread slowly by rhizomes to form clumps and can be divided every few years in spring to rejuvenate the planting and propagate new starts.
